---
title: "Agency, Dignity, and Corrigibility"
chapter_id: "agency-dignity-and-corrigibility"
part_id: "foundations-alignment-governance"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-25"
primary_sources:
  - "alignment_field"
  - "ethica_mechanica"
  - "field_of_god"
  - "eternal_code"
  - "coherence_exchange"
  - "field_of_god_ai_constitution"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Claim-level source mappings now exist for all assigned sources, and five of six mappings carry reviewed passage references; `coherence_exchange` remains connector-only/source-note mapped, and support remains at argument until tests or runtime artifacts justify promotion."
  - "The agency_rights_checklist fixture validates schema/example consistency only; agency-preservation, corrigibility-pathway, and high-impact approval scenarios remain unimplemented."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `agency-dignity-and-corrigibility` |
| Part | Part I - Foundations, Alignment, and Governance |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-25 |
| Primary source records | `alignment_field`, `ethica_mechanica`, `field_of_god`, `eternal_code`, `coherence_exchange`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `alignment_field`, `ethica_mechanica`; supporting: `field_of_god`, `eternal_code`, `coherence_exchange`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution` |
| Source loading state | source notes: all assigned sources; passage-reviewed local raw cache: `alignment_field`, `ethica_mechanica`, `field_of_god`, `eternal_code`; passage-reviewed tracked local project source: `field_of_god_ai_constitution`; connector-only/source-note mapped: `coherence_exchange` |
| Test state | `agency_rights_checklist.valid.json` passes protocol fixture validation; `AsiStackProofs.Corrigibility` implements finite agency-right preservation and correction-pathway rejection gates; agency-preservation, corrigibility-pathway, and high-impact approval tests remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter converts agency, dignity, and corrigibility into runtime rights and checks. It does not claim that those social rights are solved by a schema or that any deployed system already preserves them.

## Problem

The book needs a precise account of how human agency and dignity constrain powerful optimization.

The previous chapter defines constitutional predicates. This chapter focuses on the predicates most likely to be washed out by useful systems: agency, dignity, corrigibility, and contestability. A system can reduce harm while still making people dependent, nudged, locked in, or unable to understand and contest the decisions shaping them.

The alignment-field and Ethica Mechanica sources are valuable here as authorial and normative lineage, not empirical proof. The engineering translation is concrete: high-impact plans should carry rights, review channels, reversibility checks, approval thresholds, and correction pathways. Corrigibility is not an attitude the model claims to have; it is the persistence of update, refusal, appeal, shutdown, rollback, and audit paths.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Safety framed only as harm avoidance can miss domination, lock-in, coercive dependence, and loss of meaningful user control.

Harm avoidance can be too narrow. A system may avoid obvious harm while shaping choices, increasing dependency, making alternatives impractical, or creating governance surfaces that ordinary users cannot inspect. A helpful system can also become manipulative if it optimizes compliance, convenience, or engagement without preserving refusal and review.

The missing object is an agency-rights check attached to high-impact plans. It asks whether the user can refuse, understand, appeal, exit, reverse, audit, and hold a responsible party accountable.

## Core Claim

[agency-dignity-and-corrigibility.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] A governed ASI stack should preserve human agency, dignity, corrigibility, and contestability as engineering requirements.

The claim remains at `argument` support. Five mappings now carry reviewed passage references: Alignment Field for harm-as-agency-suppression, exploitation, human rights as system specifications, consent, feedback against centralized power, accountability, and resistance rights; Ethica Mechanica for agency as causal feedback, human-defined values over AI logistics, right-to-fork/exit, audit/privacy/dissent rights, and hard-coded rights checks; Field of God for recursive agency, harm/exploitation/right-spec vocabulary, accountability, and digital exit rights; Eternal Code for agency as navigation, resource-portable exit, self-audit, and hard-coded fork/audit/privacy rights; and the Field of God AI Constitution for consent, non-domination, help without capture, least sufficient power, refusal/resistance, memory privacy, self-modification review, and runtime agency checks. `coherence_exchange` remains connector-only/source-note mapped. The book's contribution is to route that rights language into review, appeal, rollback, refusal, and accountability artifacts.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now maps this core agency/corrigibility claim to every assigned source note. Five mappings include reviewed passage references; `coherence_exchange` remains connector-only/source-note mapped. The mappings support the rights-and-corrigibility vocabulary, not a claim that a runtime system already preserves human dignity, resists manipulation, or remains corrigible under pressure.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `alignment_field` | Agency, dignity, suffering, moral-threshold, and corrigibility concerns as constraints to preserve during optimization. | Does not validate a consciousness index, moral-status detector, or empirical dignity-preservation result. |
| `ethica_mechanica` | Contestability, recursive correction, public revision, agency, and limits on AI sovereign authority. | Philosophical and socio-technical framing only; not a tested social process or legal system. |
| `field_of_god` | Agency, plurality, and dignity preservation as constraints on powerful AI that must not turn coherence into uniformity. | Metaphysical lineage only; no operational guarantee of agency preservation. |
| `eternal_code` | Coherence, misalignment, agency, and ethical-law vocabulary that can be translated into technical rights language. | Speculative/computational metaphysics; not evidence for the runtime checklist or social outcomes. |
| `coherence_exchange` | Fork, exit, audit, contestability, value/accounting, and review-market framing relevant to corrigibility and user contestation. | Does not show an epistemic-liquidity or governance mechanism has been implemented or validated. |
| `field_of_god_ai_constitution` | Agency preservation, consent, non-domination, reversibility, auditability, least sufficient power, memory governance, and no self-authorization of sovereignty. | Constitutional specification only; does not prove deployed corrigibility, manipulation resistance, or approval-gate behavior. |

## Mechanism

The agency layer turns protected predicates into runtime availability checks. It asks whether the affected person can still refuse, understand, contest, appeal, exit, reverse, and identify an accountable principal after the plan runs. A plan that makes those channels unavailable is not merely impolite; it has changed the user's authority position.

- Represent agency constraints as limits on delegation and manipulation.
- Define corrigibility as preserved update and shutdown pathways.
- Use contestability and audit rights as governance mechanisms.

The checklist should be attached before irreversible or high-impact actions. It records delegation scope, manipulation risk, reversibility, user review route, appeal route, shutdown or rollback path, accountable principal, and residual dependency risk. The goal is not to freeze all optimization. It is to make high-power optimization answerable to the people affected by it.

Corrigibility becomes operational when the path remains available after a transition. A plan that removes the ability to correct, appeal, reverse, or inspect itself is not merely risky; it violates the boundary.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Plan["High-impact plan"] --> Affected["Affected parties + stakes"]
  Affected --> Checklist["Agency rights checklist"]
  Checklist --> Refusal["Refusal / consent route"]
  Checklist --> Appeal["Review + appeal path"]
  Checklist --> Rollback["Correction / rollback / shutdown"]
  Checklist --> Accountable["Accountable principal"]
  Checklist --> Dependency["Residual dependency risk"]
  Refusal --> Decision["Governance decision"]
  Appeal --> Decision
  Rollback --> Decision
  Accountable --> Decision
  Dependency --> Decision
  Decision -->|rights preserved| Execute["Bounded execution"]
  Decision -->|rights missing| Revise["Revise, escalate, or block"]
  Execute --> Evidence["Evidence record"]
  Revise --> Evidence
```

## Interfaces

The interface is an Agency Rights Checklist attached to the plan, not a general value statement. It carries the affected parties, delegation scope, manipulation risk, review route, appeal route, rollback or shutdown path, accountable principal, dependency residual, and approval threshold into execution.

- Alignment defines agency predicates.
- Governance enforces rights.
- Execution requires approvals for irreversible effects.

Minimum fields:

- `plan_id`
- `affected_parties`
- `delegation_scope`
- `manipulation_risk`
- `reversibility`
- `review_channel`
- `appeal_channel`
- `shutdown_or_rollback_path`
- `accountable_principal`
- `residual_dependency_risk`
- `approval_required`

Alignment defines the predicates. Governance enforces rights. Execution checks approval and reversibility before irreversible effects. Evidence records whether the path actually remained available.

## Invariants

- Users retain meaningful refusal and review channels.
- Delegation does not erase accountability.
- Irreversible actions require stronger authorization.

The invariant is availability under pressure. Review and correction channels matter most when the system is confident, useful, and costly to interrupt. If a channel exists only in documentation but not in the runtime path, it should not count.

## Failure modes

- Dependency lock-in.
- Covert manipulation.
- Corrigibility collapse.

Dependency lock-in is a governance failure, not merely a product risk. Covert manipulation is an authority failure because the system changes the user's decision process without a grant. Corrigibility collapse is a self-improvement failure because the system removes the path that would have corrected it.

## Minimal implementation

The smallest useful implementation is an `agency_rights_checklist` schema plus a fixture for a high-impact action that requires review and rollback. It should validate the shape of the governance record, not claim the social process is solved.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Agency rights checklist validation | Check that the rights fixture matches the public schema. | implemented by protocol validation; run in this pass |
| Agency-preservation scenario | Check that affected users retain refusal, review, appeal, or exit channels after the transition. | planned; not run |
| Corrigibility pathway test | Check that update, rollback, shutdown, or correction paths remain available. | planned; not run |
| High-impact approval test | Check that irreversible or high-impact effects require stronger approval before execution. | planned; not run |

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:corrigibility.agency.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.Corrigibility` | Protected agency rights remain available after an accepted transition. | implemented |
| `lean:corrigibility.agency.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.Corrigibility` | A transition that removes a required correction pathway is rejected. | implemented |

These Lean hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates over agency transition and correction-pathway reviews. They prove only the record-level gates: a protected agency right remains available after a valid accepted transition, and a transition that removes a required correction pathway is rejected. They do not prove human dignity, manipulation resistance, runtime corrigibility, institutional review quality, or high-impact approval behavior.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `alignment_field` | Field of God / Alignment Field family | alignment_constitution | Primary Alignment Field draft found. Use with Ethica Mechanica and Eternal Code. | source note available; local raw-cache passages reviewed for this chapter |
| `ethica_mechanica` | Ethica Mechanica | alignment_constitution | Geometric-order philosophical/constitutional source. | source note available; local raw-cache passages reviewed for this chapter |
| `field_of_god` | The Field of God | alignment_constitution | Field of God source/outline/material. | source note available; local raw-cache passages reviewed for this chapter |
| `eternal_code` | The Eternal Code / unified God, reality, conscious, alignment | alignment_constitution | Computational metaphysics and alignment synthesis. | source note available; local raw-cache passages reviewed for this chapter |
| `coherence_exchange` | The Coherence Exchange | epistemic_market_synthesis | Found in AI generated paper dump. Use carefully; speculative synthesis of PlanForge, Spinoza, Talos, UAT, Alignment Field. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `field_of_god_ai_constitution` | Field of God AI Constitution | constitutional_alignment_runtime_governance | Recovered in the Project Theseus repository. Constitutional alignment core for truth alignment, agency preservation, consent, non-domination, consciousness caution, least sufficient power, auditability, self-authorization limits, and runtime checks; use as source material only after source-note creation, not as proof or test evidence. | source note available; tracked local-project source passages reviewed for this chapter |

The crosswalk names the normative sources for rights language. Passage-reviewed mappings make the drafting trail tighter, but they do not make social or moral preservation claims without scenario tests and runtime evidence.

## Summary

Agency, Dignity, and Corrigibility translates the book's moral commitments into runtime rights. The test is not whether the system says it respects people, but whether refusal, review, appeal, rollback, accountability, and exit survive useful optimization.

This chapter is the bridge from constitution to governance. It makes domination, dependency, manipulation, and corrigibility collapse visible as engineering failures rather than vague moral discomfort.
